item.title: Spelling correction
item.content:encoded: I corrected a typo. I got that everybody is allowed to that. If not, I apologize.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm guessing the furniture was bought at that perfectly normal, regular old IKEA? i mean, living flesh is a hard material to come by, but wouldn't be *that* hard to assemble, right? I'm certain a store as large as that one would have the it in stock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wonderfully creepy and well executed. This is how you take a concept and run with it.

+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as described below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

It certainly has been a while since I have read this SCP. Normally, in the process of proposing crosslinks, I try to break out of Series, linking 1 to 2 and 3, 2 to 1 and 3, etc. However, SCP-1046 and SCP-1582's respective anomalies just match up so well that I'm going to go within series for this proposal. As such, I am proposing the following edits to this article:

Changing this:

SCP-1046 was discovered on 9/18/1994, after Foundation assets in the Davie Sheriff's Department followed up on reports of a "house with people parts". Investigation into the alleged house revealed SCP-1046's anomalous properties. Subsequent surgical intervention allowed agents to achieve successful containment. SCP-1046 was contained on 11/19/1994, and classified as Euclid.

To this:

SCP-1046 was discovered on 9/18/1994, after Foundation assets in the Davie Sheriff's Department followed up on reports of a "house with people parts". Investigation into the alleged house revealed SCP-1046's anomalous properties. Subsequent surgical intervention allowed agents to achieve successful containment. SCP-1046 was contained on 11/19/1994, and classified as Euclid. Similarities between SCP-1046 and SCP-1582 have been noted, and and investigation into a similar origin of the two phenomena is currently ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's now _actually_ October 2015. Where's my Nerf Salman?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I know this is a necro post and all but it's basically October 2015 and it was to perfect to pass up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, the lack of a bedroom (to wit: there's place for a person, but…) is rather subtle, I'll give you that. However, "you really do feel like part of the home" is pretty effing heavy implication that the builders/sellers knew that this house would do this to the poor guy.
Not to mention the fact that the article is started with procedures that talk about a building, then end with "portions of SCP-1046 found outside of containment are to be surgically removed". The paragraph began with procedures telling us about a building, then ends with surgery? That got my interest right quick.

You don't like it, fine, of course. But "relies too much on the ending letters rather than any actual interest descriptions"? I don't follow.

(EDIT: Sorry for the reply to a months-old comment. Done only out of bafflement and the hope to chat with Scout a bit.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel as if this article relies too much on the ending letters rather than any actual interest descriptions of odd activity. It's that you could make the entire SCP about anything, include the final letters, and still expect us to be interested in this thing. It wasn't effective for me at all. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reread this after some time, changed my vote. It's nice. Normal stuff, suddenly boom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somebody please give this man… err… house a bedroom for god's sake!
Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one's done it yet, so I will ruin it first:

a sink being clogged by an artery

In Soviet Russia, artery clogs other thing! or something

this one is fun and I upvote even as I ponder about the letters… did the owner know they were living in a gooey flesh house? Or was it always pulsating with random giblets and gizzards and such?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: well, I think that might've been bad for the internal tonal consistency of the piece. This isn't about the Foundation, this is about the guy who is now a house without a bedroom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anybody else thinks it'd be better if the letter says 'the bedroom broke down' instead of 'it vanished'? As in, the ceiling fell down or something.

Then we could have a note from confused researchers about how there has been no trace of a bedroom at all, intact ceiling or otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt this part was a bit odd;

no longer contains the bedroom.

if its supposed to be that it never had a bedroom in the first place, this sounds like it disappeared. And if that is what happened, he doesn't seem too surprised with vanishing house parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! My 4th +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No John… you are the house.

+1. Happy to put this one in the 100 club.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Read this before in draft form, but it still bears mentioning: This is really really good. It's interesting, entertaining, and scary.

The last note feels slightly iffy because I'm not sure how he wrote it - like writing ARRRRGH on the wall while you die - but I'm willing to go with it because I like the content of the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhh. House made of people feels a bit too… cliché, somehow. I don't know if there's any others quite like it… but a screaming face on the TV? Crying taps? Seems awfully over the top.

And I don't see the connection with the bedroom at all. And why would he write letters and then not send them, but think he has? And most importantly, how did he not notice the lack of a bedroom? And when he did, why not just move out? Finally, did John become the house instantly? How could he write the letter, if that was so, and if it was gradual, how the hell did he survive with his circulatory system in the sink??

It's got a good enough score for my vote not to make a difference, but to me it's just so inconsistent that I have to down vote on principle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good article, but let's bring some attention to a couple of the amazing backhanded compliments here in the discussion section. Beautiful stuff.

Well done, Roget.
I actually couldn't tell you wrote this, better than a lot of your stuff.

Not bad, but clearly meant in a friendly and sincere way.

I guess it's probably good, but I can't see how.

Breathtaking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first actually creepy article I've read in a while. It was kind of meh until the letters, but that third one was creepy as balls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very unnerving…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's because he's been made into a house. Without a bedroom. He's describing his house-feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It bothers me that something so nonsensical makes so much sense. Makes me doubt my own critical faculties.
I apreciate the clue-in, I already upped the vote, but now I'm more certain it is deserving.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bedroom is a section of the house dedicated for your exclusive private use. He doesn't get one because he IS the house now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This guy is being shuffled into his house, and all I can think of is "bedroom? wha?"
I mean, this is quite good, very Kronenberg. But what in the bloody blue blazes is the deal with the bedroom? I feel like I'm missing some giant piece of the puzzle, or a core reference that would make it all make sense, and I've got clue zero.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had the same thoughts too, but they are very different.

This one is mildly interesting in how it may have come to be. The bedroom thing kind of baffles me, but the fate of the man makes you wonder what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree it's similar to a couple of previous SCPs - but very well done. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very remniecent of 002. But also very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've also noticed that. Not necessarily bad, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, this is pretty incredible

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ikr, I guess it's probably good, but I can't see how.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: S'cool bro. Can't please everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+58

All of my wut.

This is like a more docile 002 with lolfoundation combined. Seriously:

and any organs capable of producing sound, will begin make distraught vocalizations

The ads are right, you really do feel like part of the home

several injuries sustained on my property

I don't like this. I tried rereading, but I just don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ghhhuuuuuuuh…..

There is absolutely nothing good, right or decent about this.

… + god damn 1

Edit: I now know how to make giant red letters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Side note: For some reason I'm really tickled by the idea that this guy's bedroom disappears and rather than being horrified he's just mildly inconvenienced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I am all over." Damn, that's good. There are some things on SCP that you just don't see anywhere else, a certain kind of horror and wrongness that is too subtle or strange for mainstream media or too difficult to write well, and 1046 is right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh…really? Cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking at first that this was too much like the aboriginal ant-man, but you took the concept in an entirely different direction. This is one of the most horrifying SCPs I've read in a long while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We're sorry, the Nerf Salman won't be available for purchase until October 2015.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's nasty…I love it! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whazza whozza eauuughhhh

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the point was that he was at that time driven beyond coherent thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …dammit Roget I thought I was immune to body horror by now. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good enough for an upvote overall. The notes at the end just don't sit well with me for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because I'm tickled pink to see such elegant application of 1193-ish principles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not downvoting, because the rest of it is okay, but I can't upvote because of the last letter.

Even making allowances for the fact that dismemberment and absorption by one's home would be a perspective-changer, I can't work out who he's trying to talk to or what he's trying to communicate to them; no entity that would heed "dont take me anynmore please" is going to give a damn if he feels alone, and I can't imagine any entity that would find "i taste the staleness of the wall" meaningful information. (Maybe somebody should get him some fresh, crisp new walls?)

I suppose what I'm saying is that I have a problem with the tone. It's just not the usual problem with tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can always tell Roget's SCPs from everyone else's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one is creepy! Is was odd enough in the Sandbox, but here… *shudders*
Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just… damn. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mods pls nerf Salman

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …So do the carpets match the drapes ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ Roget.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clarifidated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing; the letters found are unsent, but they read as if the writer believes he sent them. Did he keep copies?

Yeah, this is also my biggest beef with this article. A bit more clarification on this point would be really great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, Roget.
I actually couldn't tell you wrote this, better than a lot of your stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could they? The definition of biological is nothing more or less than "pertains to biology." I mean…carbon? Water? Charcoal's primarily dead plant matter; what's suitable and what wouldn't he manifest on?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure they could express common sense in constructing containment chambers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I much prefer this to the draft yesterday. My only nitpick is "All surfaces contacting the building are to be constructed out of biological material." Many things are biological in nature and if I were surface-builder guy I would have no idea what qualifies. Skin? Tree bark? Live cats?

Upvoted despite it for a solid premise and cleanly done. "You really do feel like part of the home" is an epic zinger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, creepy as hell. Could use some clean up, but this deserves an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is solid. However, there's some issues:

Faucets located within SCP-1046 will begin to produce small amounts of water during this time, despite the building not being connected to any water source. It is currently believed that this water is produced by SCP-1046's tear ducts.

NARM.

The ads are right, you really do feel like part of the home.

Man, really?

However, I am writing you this letter due to the fact that my new house contains no bedrooms.

Who moves into a house with no bedrooms?

There's a few typos and such as well. Withholding vote until this gets cleaned up a bit.
